Most temples close by 5 PM and charge small entrance fees (300-800 yen); plan visits accordingly and wear comfortable shoes for extensive walking.
Remove shoes when entering temple buildings, speak quietly, follow one-way paths marked by staff, and never touch temple artworks or gardens.
Cherry blossoms (late March-April) and autumn foliage (November) are peak seasons with massive crowds; visit in shoulder seasons for better experiences.

places.name: Osaka
places.description: Japan's vibrant second city known for its street food, nightlife, and Osaka Castle with spectacular views. The energetic atmosphere, excellent food scene (takoyaki, okonomiyaki), and modern shopping districts make it a dynamic contrast to Kyoto's serenity.

activities.detail: Iconic 16th-century fortress with panoramic city views, museum exhibits on samurai history, and beautiful grounds especially stunning during cherry blossom season.
Vibrant entertainment and dining area famous for neon signs, street food stalls, and canal-side atmosphere that captures Osaka's energetic nightlife.
Historic working-class neighborhood packed with retro charm, affordable eateries, and authentic local atmosphere reminiscent of mid-20th century Japan.
Comprehensive museum covering 1,500 years of Osaka's past with interactive exhibits and a reconstructed Edo-period street on the ground floor.
Modern architectural landmark with an open-air rooftop observation deck offering 360-degree views of the city and surrounding Kansai region.
Traditional wholesale food market operating since 1820, offering fresh seafood, produce, and prepared foods where you can eat directly from vendors.

food.detail: Savory Japanese pancake layered with cabbage, proteins, and special sauce, cooked on a griddle and eaten with a spatula—Osaka's most iconic comfort food.
Golden-fried octopus balls made with batter and topped with takoyaki sauce, mayo, bonito flakes, and seaweed—a beloved street snack perfected in Osaka.
Thick wheat noodle soup topped with sweet fried tofu pouches (aburaage), traditionally served hot in Osaka's noodle shops.
Deep-fried skewered meat, seafood, and vegetables with a crispy panko coating, dipped in tangy brown sauce—signature Osaka dish found in Shinsekai.
Modern dessert twist featuring sweet octopus-shaped ice cream, available at shops throughout Dotonbori and night markets.
Complete dining experience pairing okonomiyaki with rice, miso soup, and pickles at traditional okonomiyaki-ya restaurants throughout the city.

culture.detail: Osaka residents are famously frank and direct compared to other Japanese cities; casual, friendly banter with vendors and locals is expected and appreciated.
Despite the energetic atmosphere, Japanese orderliness applies—always queue properly at stations, restaurants, and attractions without cutting in line.
While modernizing, many traditional food stalls, small restaurants, and markets in Shinsekai and Kuromon still only accept cash, so carry yen.
Osaka's liveliest dining and entertainment scene activates after 6 PM; plan major food experiences and nightlife activities for evening hours when the city truly comes alive.

places.name: Kobe
places.description: Elegant port city famous for Kobe beef, historic foreign settlements, and views of Osaka Bay framed by mountains. The blend of international atmosphere, culinary excellence, and scenic coastal beauty makes it one of Japan's most cosmopolitan destinations.

activities.detail: A waterfront promenade featuring the iconic Kobe Port Tower and Kawasaki Good Time World, offering panoramic views of Osaka Bay and the surrounding mountains.
A collection of restored Western-style mansions from the Meiji era showcasing the city's international heritage, with museums, cafes, and shops housed in historic buildings.
One of Japan's oldest hot spring resorts located in the hills north of Kobe, famous for its mineral-rich waters and traditional ryokan experiences.
A scenic hiking spot accessible by cable car offering views of Kobe's urban landscape with traditional Japanese waterfall gardens.
A modern shopping and entertainment district with waterfront dining, designer boutiques, and evening views of the illuminated port skyline.
One of Japan's oldest zoos nestled in forested hills, home to over 130 animal species with natural enclosures and scenic walking paths.

food.detail: Japan's most prestigious beef, graded for its marbling and tenderness, best enjoyed as thin slices in yakiniku or as steaks at specialist restaurants.
Local-style ramen featuring rich soy-based broth with seafood dashi, often topped with pork belly and green onions, distinct from Kyoto or Osaka versions.
Takoyaki-like octopus balls unique to nearby Akashi, with a more delicate egg batter and dashi broth for dipping rather than takoyaki sauce.
Kobe's Nada ward produces one-third of Japan's sake; distillery visits offer tastings and traditional brewing technique demonstrations.
Luxury cantaloupe melons from local orchards, often presented as premium gifts, with sweet, aromatic flesh that represents Kobe's agricultural heritage.

culture.detail: Kobe's international character means casual Western dress is common, but remove shoes when entering traditional establishments, temples, or homes.
The 1995 Great Hanshin Earthquake deeply shaped Kobe's identity; respectful acknowledgment of the disaster and its resilient reconstruction is culturally appropriate when learning about the city.
Explore narrow yokocho (alley) areas like Kobe Shinkaichi or Honcho-dori for intimate local restaurants and standing bars; these informal spaces are where locals truly gather.

places.name: Arashiyama
places.description: Kyoto's most iconic bamboo grove and scenic mountain district featuring the famous Arashiyama Bamboo Forest and Togetsukyo Bridge. This picturesque area offers serene nature, traditional gardens, and temples surrounded by breathtaking seasonal beauty.

activities.detail: Walk through towering bamboo stalks on a peaceful path that creates an otherworldly green tunnel, best visited early morning to avoid crowds.
Cross this iconic wooden bridge spanning the Oigawa River with stunning views of the surrounding mountains and seasonal foliage.
Explore this UNESCO World Heritage temple with its remarkable rock and pond garden reflecting the Arashiyama peaks, including access to the bamboo grove.
Visit this former silent film star's mansion featuring traditional gardens, tea house, and panoramic views of Kyoto valley from the mountain ridge.
Ride an open-air trolley through the Sagano district with views of the Hozu River gorge and bamboo forests, with seasonal variations and evening illumination options.
Climb to this hilltop sanctuary to see Japanese wild monkeys in their natural habitat while enjoying expansive views of Kyoto.

food.detail: Enjoy silky tofu cooked in a kombu broth at specialized restaurants, a Kyoto specialty particularly popular in the temple district where it originated as Buddhist vegetarian cuisine.
Taste this seasonal summer delicacy prepared as sashimi or in hot pot, prized in Kyoto for its delicate flavor and traditionally associated with the Gion and Arashiyama culinary traditions.
Sample handmade buckwheat noodles served either hot or cold, a staple lunch dish at numerous shops throughout Arashiyama reflecting Kyoto's noodle-making heritage.
Indulge in green tea sweets, soft serve ice cream, and traditional wagashi cakes available at tea houses and shops, often enjoyed alongside ceremonial tea in the bamboo forest area.
Experience multi-course haute cuisine featuring seasonal Kyoto ingredients and artistic presentation at upscale restaurants overlooking the river and mountains.

culture.detail: Arrive at the bamboo forest before 7 AM to experience tranquility and avoid the large tour groups that dominate midday hours.
Remove shoes when entering temple buildings, speak quietly, and follow posted photography restrictions as many areas remain active spiritual spaces.
Wear comfortable walking shoes and bring layers as Arashiyama's elevation and bamboo create cooler microclimates; seasonal weather can change rapidly on the hillside.
Spring cherry blossom season and autumn foliage peak times draw massive crowds, so consider visiting in winter or early summer for a more authentic experience.

places.name: Nara
places.description: Ancient capital home to the massive bronze Daibutsu (Great Buddha) in Todai-ji Temple and hundreds of friendly, bowing deer roaming Nara Park. This UNESCO World Heritage site offers remarkable temples, spacious parks, and a relaxed atmosphere perfect for a day trip from Kyoto.

activities.detail: Marvel at the colossal 15-meter bronze Great Buddha, one of the world's largest metal statues, housed in the massive wooden temple hall.
Feed and interact with over 1,000 semi-wild sika deer roaming freely through the 660-hectare park, where they bow for food and pose for photos.
Walk through thousands of red lanterns lining the approach to this atmospheric Shinto shrine nestled against forested hillsides.
Explore Buddhist artifacts and historical treasures housed adjacent to the Great Buddha, providing context for the temple's significance.
Discover layered temple buildings and pagodas representing different periods of Japanese Buddhist architecture throughout the expansive grounds.
Stroll through preserved wooden merchant houses and narrow lanes in this charming old town quarter filled with shops, cafes, and galleries.
Climb to this hillside hall in the Todai-ji complex for panoramic sunset views over the park and city below.

food.detail: Persimmon leaf-wrapped sushi parcels with pressed fish, a traditional Nara specialty that preserves flavors and offers natural antimicrobial benefits.
Vegetables pickled in sake lees in a distinctive light brown color, offering a sweet and umami-rich taste unique to the region.
Sample locally-grown green tea from the Yamato region, known for its refreshing quality and subtle sweetness.
Soft rice cakes wrapped in persimmon leaves, combining chewy texture with a delicate floral note from the leaf packaging.
Shaved ice topped with roasted soybean powder and syrup, a refreshing summer treat enjoyed throughout the park.

culture.detail: Purchase special deer crackers from vendors rather than offering your own food, and bow slightly when feeding to encourage the traditional 'bowing deer' behavior.
Always check signage before photographing inside temple halls, as many inner sanctums prohibit cameras to maintain spiritual atmosphere and protect artwork.
Visit Kasuga Taisha in early February (Setsubun) or mid-August (Obon) when thousands of lanterns are lit, creating a magical evening atmosphere.
Arrive early at major temples before 9 AM to experience a more serene atmosphere, avoid crowds, and potentially witness monks' morning rituals.

places.name: Hiroshima
places.description: Home to the poignant Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park and Museum dedicated to WWII history and promoting peace. The site is deeply meaningful, offering important historical context and beautiful riverside gardens that inspire reflection on humanity.

activities.detail: Walk through the iconic park centered on the Peace Memorial Monument, with the haunting A-Bomb Dome ruins and reflecting pools that honor the victims of the 1945 atomic bombing.
Explore the comprehensive museum documenting the history of the atomic bomb, wartime Hiroshima, and the city's recovery through photographs, artifacts, and personal testimonies.
Visit the famous floating torii gate and sacred shrine on nearby Miyajima Island, one of Japan's most photographed sites accessible by a short ferry ride from central Hiroshima.
Climb the reconstructed five-story castle with panoramic views of the city and exhibits on samurai history and the castle's role through different eras.
Stroll through this serene 16th-century landscape garden featuring miniaturized mountain and valley scenes, perfect for quiet reflection.
Wander through the lively riverside entertainment quarter with restaurants, bars, and street art that showcase modern Hiroshima's vibrant cultural rebirth.

food.detail: Try the local savory pancake layered with noodles, cabbage, egg, and your choice of protein, cooked on a flat griddle and served with thick Hiroshima-style sauce and bonito flakes.
Savor this regional specialty of grilled conger eel served over rice in a wooden box, often prepared at local restaurants in the Nagarekawa area.
Enjoy noodles in a rich broth topped with local ingredients like bean sprouts, pork, and a soft-boiled egg, reflecting the region's post-war street food traditions.
Experience finely shredded chicken and egg over rice, a simple but flavorful Hiroshima comfort food found at casual eateries throughout the city.
Taste the famous maple-leaf-shaped sponge cakes filled with red bean paste or other fillings, a classic souvenir particularly associated with Miyajima Island near Hiroshima.

culture.text: Respectful Behavior at the Peace Memorial
culture.detail: Maintain quiet, contemplative behavior in the Peace Memorial Park and Museum—this is a sacred space of remembrance where photography inside the museum is often restricted out of respect for the deceased.
Plan visits to popular sites like Miyajima's torii gate early in the morning or on weekdays to avoid peak tourist crowds, especially during cherry blossom season and summer holidays.
Purchase an IC card like Paspy at convenience stores for seamless travel on trams and buses throughout Hiroshima, as the tram system is the primary local transit method.

If visiting in August, be aware that Hiroshima holds commemorative peace events around August 6th, and the city also celebrates the Hiroshima Flower Festival in May with street processions and food stalls.

places.name: Miyajima
places.description: Sacred island famous for the iconic floating torii gate of Itsukushima Shrine, which appears to float on water at high tide. One of Japan's most photographed locations, it offers spiritual atmosphere, beautiful nature walks, and excellent local cuisine.

activities.detail: Visit the iconic vermillion torii gate that appears to float on water at high tide, situated within this UNESCO World Heritage shrine dating back to 1168.
Climb or cable-car up to the 530-meter peak for panoramic views of the Seto Inland Sea and surrounding islands, with a scenic forest trail.
Explore this 1200-year-old Buddhist temple on Mount Misen's slopes, featuring a five-story pagoda, peaceful gardens, and intricate interior sculptures.
Stroll the charming pedestrian arcade lined with traditional shops, restaurants, and cafes selling local crafts, souvenirs, and regional specialties.
Walk through scenic gardens famous for Japanese maple trees that turn brilliant red in autumn, offering seasonal photography and peaceful nature trails.
Learn about the island's 1400-year spiritual history, shrine traditions, and local culture through artifacts, documents, and traditional displays.
Visit this large wooden stage building built in 1587 that sits adjacent to the torii gate, originally intended as a Buddhist hall and now a cultural landmark.

food.detail: Miyajima's signature dish featuring tender grilled freshwater conger eel glazed with a sweet soy sauce and served over warm white rice.
Delicate small cakes shaped like maple leaves and filled with red bean paste or other fillings, a popular souvenir that represents the island's autumn beauty.
A hearty layered savory pancake made with cabbage, noodles, egg, and meat stacked and cooked together, finished with okonomiyaki sauce and bonito flakes.
Fresh oysters from the Seto Inland Sea grilled and served with soy sauce or ponzu, showcasing the region's renowned shellfish harvesting tradition.
Small sweet clams harvested locally and served grilled or in miso soup, known for their delicate flavor and regional availability.

culture.detail: Plan your torii gate photos around high tide (approximately 6 hours after the previous low tide) when the gate appears to float, as it sits on mudflats at low tide.
Miyajima is considered sacred ground; avoid pointing at the torii gate directly, remove shoes when entering shrine areas, and be respectful of ongoing religious ceremonies.
Visit early morning (before 8am) or late afternoon to avoid peak tourist crowds, especially during maple leaf season (November) and summer holidays when day-trippers arrive en masse.
Due to the island's sacred status, historically no deaths or cremations have been permitted on Miyajima, reflecting its spiritual significance in Shinto tradition.

places.name: Koyasan
places.description: Sacred mountain monastery complex and UNESCO site featuring Kongobuji Temple and the atmospheric Okunoin cemetery shrouded in towering cryptomeria trees. Visitors can experience Buddhist monk life through shukubo temple stays, vegetarian cuisine, and meditation in this spiritually significant location.

activities.detail: Wander through the atmospheric two-kilometer path lined with towering cryptomeria trees and 200,000 moss-covered graves, ending at the mausoleum of Kobo Daishi.
Explore the chief temple of Koyasan featuring ornate halls, the famous rock garden, and intricate Buddhist artwork representing the Shingon sect.
Spend a night in a working monastery participating in dawn prayers, meditation, and receiving Buddhist teachings directly from monks.
Visit this ancient temple complex with its iconic Kondo hall and five-story pagoda representing the mandala cosmology of Shingon Buddhism.
Discover treasures including Buddhist sculptures, paintings, and calligraphy spanning over 1,200 years of religious and artistic heritage.
Experience the magical sight of 10,000 stone lanterns illuminated throughout Okunoin cemetery during this annual two-day Buddhist memorial event.

activities.text: Mount Koya Cable Car and Forest Walks
activities.detail: Ride the scenic ropeway up the mountain or hike forest trails between temples to appreciate the natural mountain sanctuary.

food.detail: Elaborate multi-course vegetarian Buddhist cuisine prepared by temple cooks using seasonal mountain vegetables, tofu, and traditional preparation methods.
Crispy battered burdock root—a local specialty that exemplifies Koyasan's vegetarian temple cuisine tradition.
Creamy sesame tofu dessert with a nutty flavor and delicate texture, commonly served as a final course in temple meals.

food.text: Koyasan Sake Brewery Tour and Tasting
food.detail: Visit local sake breweries on the mountain to sample sake crafted using Koyasan's pure spring water and learn traditional brewing methods.
Buckwheat noodles topped with grated mountain yam, a light and refreshing dish suited to the mountain climate and vegetarian diet.

culture.detail: Always remove footwear when entering temple halls, meditation rooms, and accommodation areas as a sign of respect and spiritual purification.
Speak softly and move deliberately throughout the sacred grounds, particularly in Okunoin cemetery where visitors are walking among the deceased.
If staying overnight, join all scheduled activities including early morning prayer services; wearing provided robes and maintaining meditation posture demonstrates cultural respect.

culture.text: Purchase Omamori and Pay Respects Properly
culture.detail: Buy protective amulets at temple shops and understand that leaving offerings at graves and altars follows Buddhist practice—ask monks for proper etiquette.

places.name: Osaka Castle
places.description: Iconic five-tiered castle with striking gold leaf accents overlooking the city, reconstructed with modern interiors housing a museum. Set within a spacious park with seasonal cherry blossoms, it offers panoramic city views and insight into Japan's feudal history.

activities.text: Explore the Main Keep Interior Museum
activities.detail: Climb through eight floors of exhibits displaying samurai armor, weapons, historical documents, and dioramas depicting Osaka Castle's role in unifying Japan under Toyotomi Hideyoshi.

activities.text: Walk the Stone Walls and Moats
activities.detail: Circumnavigate the castle's massive defensive perimeter featuring original Edo-period stonework and multiple concentric moats that once protected the fortress.

activities.text: Cherry Blossom Viewing in Osaka Castle Park
activities.detail: During late March to early April, enjoy hanami season among approximately 3,000 cherry trees surrounding the castle, with evening illuminations highlighting the blooms.
Stroll through this traditional Japanese garden featuring seasonal flowers, tea houses, and views framing the main castle keep, especially beautiful in autumn.
Ascend to the upper floors of the main keep for 360-degree panoramic views of Osaka's skyline and the surrounding Kansai region.
Experience the castle illuminated nightly with gold-accented LED lighting that dramatically highlights the structure's five tiers and architectural details.

food.detail: Osaka's signature savory pancake layered with cabbage, meat or seafood, egg, and topped with sweet-savory okonomiyaki sauce, mayo, bonito flakes, and aonori powder.
Golden-fried octopus balls originating from Osaka, filled with tender takoyaki and served with takoyaki sauce, mayo, bonito, and aonori at street vendors throughout the park area.
Thick wheat noodles in savory broth topped with sweet fried tofu (aburaage), a Kansai specialty easily found in noodle shops near the castle.
Just outside the castle grounds, the vibrant Dotonbori entertainment district offers kaiseki restaurants, casual ramen shops, and fresh sushi alongside the Okawa River.
Grilled chicken skewers paired with local sake represent traditional Osaka casual dining, available at izakayas throughout the castle area.

culture.detail: While the exterior and park allow photography, certain museum exhibits and historical artifacts prohibit flash photography or tripods—observe signage and fellow visitors' conduct.
Some interior museum sections, particularly areas displaying tatami-floored traditional rooms, require shoe removal; plan accordingly and wear easily removable footwear.
Osaka Castle attracts over 2 million visitors annually; arriving before 9 AM or visiting on weekdays ensures shorter queues and a more contemplative experience.
The castle represents both Toyotomi Hideyoshi's unification of Japan and the Tokugawa shogunate's power; grasping this duality enriches understanding of exhibits and Japan's feudal period.

places.name: Fushimi
places.description: Historic sake brewing district in Kyoto known for sake museums, breweries open to visitors, and the atmospheric Fushimi Inari shrine with thousands of vermilion torii gates. The charming riverside area blends culinary heritage with spiritual atmosphere and excellent sake tastings.

activities.detail: Explore thousands of vermilion torii gates winding up the sacred mountain in this iconic Shinto shrine dedicated to the kami of rice and commerce.
Tour this historic sake brewery's museum to learn about 370+ years of sake production and sample different varieties in the tasting room.
Visit one of Fushimi's working breweries to see the sake-making process firsthand and taste limited-edition brews from the barrel.
Walk the scenic riverside paths lined with willows, traditional warehouses, and small galleries in the atmospheric Fushimi Sake Brewery District.
Discover whimsical kappa folklore and contemporary sake brewing at this unique museum dedicated to the mischievous water spirits of Fushimi.

activities.text: Canal boat tour of Meiji Restoration waterway
activities.detail: Take a short pleasure boat ride through the historic canal that once transported sake barrels from breweries to Osaka during the Edo period.

food.detail: Sample flights of different sake grades and styles directly at brewery tasting rooms, from dry junmai to floral ginjo varieties.
Fresh local river fish or sea bream steamed with sake and served at riverside restaurants to highlight Fushimi's brewing heritage.
Soft tofu, eggs, and vegetables simmered in a rich dashi broth enriched with local sake, a warming specialty found in traditional restaurants.
Delicate arrowroot jelly sweets made with sake or sake lees, often served chilled as a sophisticated palate cleanser between tastings.
High-end sushi restaurants use the pure groundwater that feeds the sake breweries, believed to enhance the subtle flavors of raw fish and rice.

culture.detail: Many breweries require advance reservations and have specific tasting hours; plan visits for mid-morning to early afternoon when tours are most active.

culture.text: Respect quiet hours at Inari shrine
culture.detail: Early morning visits to Fushimi Inari before 8 AM offer the most serene experience with fewer crowds and better spiritual atmosphere.
Taste sake in small ceramic cups at room temperature or slightly chilled to appreciate subtle flavors; asking brewers about aging and water sources shows genuine interest.
While photos are welcome, avoid blocking paths or touching gates; the most photogenic spots fill quickly after sunrise, so arrive early for solitude.

places.name: Takayama
places.description: Beautifully preserved Edo-period merchant town in the Japanese Alps featuring sake breweries, morning markets, and traditional wooden houses lining atmospheric streets. It offers quintessential old Japan aesthetics with excellent local crafts, cuisine, and access to surrounding mountain scenery.

activities.detail: Stroll the three beautifully preserved merchant streets lined with wooden machiya houses, sake breweries, and traditional shops dating back to the Edo period.
Tour the only surviving samurai government building in Japan, featuring original tatami rooms, torture chamber, and administrative offices from the Edo period.
Visit either Takayama Jinya Morning Market or Miyagawa Morning Market to browse local vegetables, flowers, crafts, and seasonal goods from local farmers.
Sample and purchase local sake at working breweries like Hirase or Fujii Sake Brewery, many offering tastings and explanations of the traditional brewing process.
Experience a nostalgic museum recreating post-war 1960s Japanese street life with vintage shops, games, and authentic period atmosphere.
Explore an open-air museum displaying 25 traditional gassho-zukuri farmhouses and workshops showcasing regional crafts and daily life from the Hida region.

food.detail: Sample the local wagyu specialty, grilled at traditional restaurants or served in sukiyaki, renowned for its marbling and tenderness from cattle raised in the mountain region.
Eat thin noodles in a rich soy-based pork broth topped with chashu pork, local to the Hida region with a distinct salty and savory flavor profile.
Enjoy seasonal mountain vegetables like bamboo shoots, ferns, and mushrooms prepared simply to highlight their fresh, delicate flavors in local restaurants.
Try local Hida miso used in dishes like miso soup, miso-grilled foods, and as a dipping sauce, made from traditional fermentation methods unique to the region.
Taste Takayama's award-winning sake varieties at brewery tasting rooms, paired with local snacks or enjoyed at izakayas throughout the historic district.

culture.detail: Visit between 7-10 AM when markets are most vibrant and vendor selection is best; many stalls close by mid-morning as goods sell out.
Always ask permission before photographing shopkeepers, brewery owners, or individuals; some sake breweries may restrict interior photos.
Popular traditional restaurants and izakayas in Sanmachi Suji fill quickly, especially for dinner; booking ahead ensures access to authentic local dining experiences.
Plan visits around Takayama Matsuri in April and October when elaborate floats parade through town; the town becomes crowded but offers authentic cultural celebrations.

places.name: Shirakawa-go
places.description: Remote mountain village famous for distinctive gassho-zukuri farmhouses with steep thatched roofs designed to withstand heavy snow. Designated a UNESCO World Heritage site, it preserves traditional agricultural life and offers magical seasonal views especially enchanting in winter.

activities.detail: Stroll through the main preserved settlement to admire around 150 gassho-zukuri farmhouses and experience the authentic mountain village atmosphere.
Soak in a traditional onsen bath overlooking the village and surrounding mountains, often with snow-covered scenery in winter.
Explore a collection of relocated historic farmhouses to learn about traditional construction, daily life, and agricultural practices of the region.
Hike to the hilltop overlook for panoramic views of the entire village nestled in the valley, especially magical at sunset or after snowfall.
Sample locally-brewed cloudy sake (doburoku) at traditional breweries, a regional specialty made with mountain spring water.
Attend spring and autumn festivals featuring traditional performances, food stalls, and rituals unique to Shirakawa-go's agricultural calendar.

food.detail: Grilled miso paste, vegetables, and mushrooms cooked on a magnolia leaf over a charcoal fire, a hearty traditional mountain dish.
Hand-pulled buckwheat noodles served hot or cold, made fresh using local spring water and often topped with mountain vegetables.
Local beef simmered with seasonal vegetables and tofu in a communal pot, warming and deeply flavored.
Fresh river fish caught from mountain streams, grilled whole and seasoned simply with salt to showcase delicate flavor.
Soft mochi dumplings made with local ingredients, often served with sweet soy glaze or miso, sold at village shops.

culture.detail: Many residences are private homes, not museums; always ask permission before photographing people or their farmhouses, especially during winter when visitors increase.
Heavy snow closes roads frequently from December to March; check weather and road conditions beforehand, and consider visiting early morning for the most serene atmosphere.
This is a working village where residents still live and farm; keep noise low, avoid eating while walking, and shop at village establishments to support the local economy.
The steep thatched roofs were designed to shed heavy mountain snow and allow silkworm farming in the attic; understanding this history enriches appreciation of the architecture.

places.name: Kumano Kodo and Nachi Falls
places.description: Ancient pilgrimage trails through sacred forest leading to one of Japan's most stunning waterfalls surrounded by lush wilderness. This UNESCO World Heritage site offers spiritual trekking, pristine nature, and access to remote Kumano shrines in a dramatic mountainous setting.

activities.detail: Trek the most popular 2–3 day Kumano Kodo route through old-growth forest with stone steps, passing through villages and arriving at Kumano Hongu Taisha.
Witness Japan's tallest single-drop waterfall at 133 meters, best viewed from the observation platform near the vermillion pagoda of Seigantoji temple.
Pray at this sacred shrine dedicated to the waterfall itself, perched on a mountainside with ancient wooden structures and stunning forest views.
Soak in naturally heated waters at traditional inns and public baths throughout Kii Peninsula, many fed by geothermal springs near the trails.
Visit the grand shrine at the convergence of three sacred rivers, featuring a massive torii gate and peaceful riverside setting steeped in pilgrimage history.
Hike this scenic mountain pass on the Nakahechi route offering panoramic views of forested valleys and traditional waymarkers (stone cairns) along the ancient trail.
Photograph the iconic vermillion three-story pagoda framed by Nachi Falls, an UNESCO-listed structure that has stood as a symbol of Kumano pilgrimage for centuries.

food.detail: Local specialty of raw oysters wrapped in persimmon or oak leaves, reflecting Kii Peninsula's coastal tradition and unique regional ingredients.
Mountain greens and wild plants foraged from the forest and prepared as simple tempura or simmered dishes at village restaurants near the trails.
Citrus-flavored broths, ponzu sauces, and preserved yuzu from local groves, commonly served with fish and in noodle soups at Kii Peninsula restaurants.

food.text: Jibuni (mountain chicken or wild boar stew)
food.detail: Rustic one-pot dish of locally hunted game simmered with local vegetables, a hearty meal available at traditional inns and village eateries.
Kii Peninsula's famous salted plums used as trail snacks or served with rice, prized for their digestive properties on long pilgrimages.

culture.text: Respect the sacred nature of shrines
culture.detail: Remove shoes before entering shrine buildings, bow before passing through torii gates, and observe silence in meditation areas as a sign of reverence for the pilgrimage tradition.

culture.text: Book accommodations (minshuku or ryokan) in advance
culture.detail: Rural mountain villages have limited lodging and meals are often pre-arranged set menus; advance booking ensures availability and authentic home-cooked experiences.
Purchase or bring this passport-style booklet to collect stamps at each shrine and lodge along the trail, a meaningful keepsake of your spiritual journey.

culture.text: Follow Leave No Trace principles strictly
culture.detail: Pack out all waste, stay on marked trails, and avoid disturbing forest wildlife; Kumano's pristine ecosystem depends on pilgrims respecting its sacred wilderness status.

places.name: Kanazawa
places.description: Castle town with stunning Kenrokuen Garden, traditional geisha districts, and the contemporary 21st Century Museum of Art. The city perfectly balances historical preservation with modern culture and offers exquisite kaiseki dining, gold leaf crafts, and access to nearby hot springs.

activities.detail: One of Japan's three great gardens, featuring meticulously landscaped paths, ponds, tea houses, and seasonal flora spanning 13 acres.
Former samurai castle grounds with reconstructed gates and walls overlooking the city, adjacent to Kenrokuen and ideal for walks.
Contemporary art museum with free outdoor installations, interactive exhibits, and the famous blue pool installation accessible to the public.
Beautifully preserved geisha quarter with traditional wooden machiya houses, teahouses, and shops selling gold leaf products and local crafts.
A 17th-century temple famous for its hidden passageways, trap doors, and complex interior layout designed to confound intruders.
The western geisha district offering an intimate alternative to Higashi with traditional teahouses, sake breweries, and fewer crowds.
Dedicated museum showcasing Kanazawa's centuries-old tradition of gold leaf craftsmanship with displays of decorated objects and artisan demonstrations.

food.detail: Fresh sushi featuring local ingredients like seabream, shrimp, and seasonal catch from the nearby Sea of Japan prepared in traditional style.
Multi-course haute cuisine dinner featuring seasonal mountain and sea ingredients served in elegant traditional settings throughout the city.
A local side dish of sautéed burdock root and carrot with a slightly sweet and savory sauce, commonly served in traditional restaurants.

food.text: Gold Leaf Ice Cream and Sweets
food.detail: Local specialty confections and desserts topped with edible 24-karat gold leaf, reflecting Kanazawa's renowned metalwork tradition.
Kaga region hot pot dish with local vegetables, duck or chicken, and seasoned broth cooked at the table.
Local curry style served over rice or with bread, less spicy than Indian versions and incorporating regional vegetables and flavors.

culture.detail: When attending a geisha performance or tea ceremony, silence and respectful observation are expected; photography is typically prohibited during performances.
Gold leaf products are fragile and considered precious; handle with care and never touch the gold directly with bare fingers to avoid transferring oils.
Major attractions have specific opening hours that vary seasonally; plan visits early in the day to avoid crowds and to fully experience gardens in morning light.
Nearby hot springs require bathing etiquette: wash thoroughly before entering the communal bath, and do not bring towels into the water itself.

restaurants.name: Kiji
restaurants.description: Historic okonomiyaki institution since 1945, perfecting layered savory pancakes with theatrical teppan cooking.

restaurants.name: Mizuno
restaurants.description: Michelin-starred seasonal kaiseki showcasing Kansai's finest ingredients through meticulous multi-course progression.

restaurants.name: Harukoma Sushi
restaurants.description: Standing sushi counter delivering fresh nigiri at reasonable prices, beloved by locals for quality and speed.

restaurants.name: Yoshimi
restaurants.description: Intimate Kyoto kaiseki with 8-seat counter, emphasizing Kyo-yasai vegetables and seasonal Yodo River fish.

restaurants.name: Gion Tanto
restaurants.description: Gion sukiyaki specialist cooking premium Kobe beef at table, intimate wooden machiya setting.

restaurants.name: Ippudo Ramen
restaurants.description: Chain-origin legendary tonkotsu ramen from Fukuoka tradition, creamy pork broth and springy noodles at wallet-friendly prices.

restaurants.name: Tempura Kondo
restaurants.description: Counter tempura featuring seasonal seafood and vegetables fried to light, crisp perfection by skilled artisans.

restaurants.name: Hyotei
restaurants.description: Centuries-old temple yudofu specialist serving silken tofu simmered in kelp broth in serene garden setting.

restaurants.name: Kobe Beef Steak Mouriya
restaurants.description: Premium Kobe beef steakhouse with teppan cooking at table, sourcing certified A5 marbled cattle.

restaurants.name: Osaka Kushikatsu Daruma
restaurants.description: Iconic deep-fried skewer specialist with panko-crusted meats, vegetables, and seafood served with tangy dipping sauce.

restaurants.name: Menami
restaurants.description: Relaxed kaiseki emphasizing fresh sashimi and seasonal mountain vegetables in elegant but approachable courses.

restaurants.name: Umenoki
restaurants.description: Traditional unagi restaurant grilling whole eels over charcoal, gloss-finishing with proprietary sweet-salty sauce.

restaurants.name: Ajisai
restaurants.description: Omakase sushi counter featuring rare Seto Inland Sea fish and careful rice temperature management.

restaurants.name: Gion Inoda Coffee
restaurants.description: Historic Gion coffee shop since 1975 serving excellent pour-over coffee and tonkatsu sandwiches in atmospheric wooden interior.

restaurants.name: Dotombori Konamon Museum
restaurants.description: Famous takoyaki stall producing piping-hot octopus balls with creamy interior, drizzled with mayo and bonito flakes.

regions/chugoku.json.name: Chūgoku
regions/chugoku.json.history: The Chūgoku region, comprising the five prefectures of Hiroshima, Okayama, Shimane, Tottori, and Yamaguchi, has a rich medieval history shaped by powerful daimyo clans and strategic positioning between Honshu's interior and the Seto Inland Sea. During the Edo period, the region became economically prosperous through castle towns and maritime trade, with Hiroshima emerging as a significant political and cultural center. The region's modern history was forever marked by the atomic bombing of Hiroshima in 1945, an event that transformed the city into a symbol of peace and remembrance. Post-war Chūgoku has rebuilt itself as an important industrial and cultural hub in western Japan.
regions/chugoku.json.culturalNotes: Chūgoku is known for its distinctive blend of samurai heritage and merchant culture, visible in well-preserved castle towns and historical neighborhoods like those in Kurashiki and Takayama. The region has a strong tradition of craftsmanship, producing famous ceramics, sake, and textiles, while its proximity to the Seto Inland Sea has fostered a unique maritime culture. Local festivals and traditions emphasize community bonds and seasonal celebrations, reflecting the region's deep connection to nature and historical continuity.
regions/chugoku.json.bestTimeToVisit: Spring and autumn offer ideal weather for sightseeing and exploring historical sites.
regions/chugoku.json.languageNotes: The Chūgoku dialect, part of the Western Japanese language group, features distinctive phonetic characteristics and vocabulary that differ noticeably from standard Tokyo Japanese. Visitors will find that standard Japanese works well for communication, though locals may appreciate efforts to understand regional phrases and expressions unique to their prefectures.

places.name: Hiroshima
places.description: Japan's most historically significant city, home to the Peace Memorial Park and Museum documenting the 1945 atomic bombing and its aftermath. A poignant and essential destination that combines solemn remembrance with vibrant modern urban culture.

activities.detail: The central memorial complex documenting the atomic bombing of August 6, 1945, with exhibits, the iconic A-Bomb Dome, and cenotaph for victims.
A reconstructed 16th-century feudal castle offering panoramic city views and exhibits on samurai history and the castle's role through WWII.
A UNESCO World Heritage Shinto shrine famous for its floating torii gate, located on nearby Miyajima Island accessible by train and ferry.
Houses a diverse collection of Western and Japanese art, including works responding to war and peace themes relevant to the city's history.
A dedicated alley with 24 traditional okonomiyaki restaurants where locals gather to watch chefs prepare Hiroshima's signature layered savory pancakes.
A serene Edo-period landscape garden featuring a central pond, walking paths, and tea house, offering peaceful respite from urban energy.

food.detail: A layered savory pancake with cabbage, noodles, eggs, and meat or seafood, cooked on a flat griddle and topped with okonomiyaki sauce and bonito flakes—distinct from Osaka's mixed version.
Grilled fresh conger eel glazed with soy-based sauce served over steamed rice, a regional specialty showcasing seafood from the Seto Inland Sea.
Fresh local sushi featuring seasonal catches from nearby waters, often with distinctive preparations using Hiroshima's seafood heritage.
A maple leaf-shaped sponge cake filled with red bean paste or custard cream, a popular souvenir from Miyajima Island.
Hiroshima prefecture produces premium sake; sake bars and brewery tours offer tastings of local varieties with guidance from knowledgeable staff.

culture.text: Respectful behavior at Peace Memorial sites
culture.detail: Maintain quiet, contemplative demeanor in the museum and park; photography is permitted but visitors should be sensitive to the solemnity of the space and other visitors' reflections.
Sit at the counter to watch chefs work and engage in casual conversation; tipping is not customary, and it's acceptable to eat directly from the shared plate or transfer to your own.

Hiroshima hosts major festivals like the Hiroshima Flower Festival in spring and Peace Memorial events in August; visiting during these times offers deeper cultural immersion but requires advance planning for accommodations.

Locals are known for warmth and directness; learning a few basic Japanese phrases is appreciated, and many residents in tourism areas speak some English but may not assume visitors understand Japanese customs.

places.name: Miyajima
places.description: Home to the iconic floating torii gate of Itsukushima Shrine, one of Japan's most photographed landmarks that appears to float on water during high tide. The island is a UNESCO World Heritage site offering temples, hiking trails, and stunning views across the Seto Inland Sea.

activities.text: Itsukushima Shrine and Floating Torii Gate
activities.detail: Visit Japan's most iconic shrine with its famous vermillion torii gate that appears to float on water during high tide, a UNESCO World Heritage site.
Trek to the 535-meter summit via forest trails for panoramic views of the Seto Inland Sea and surrounding islands, or take the ropeway for a shorter ascent.
Explore this important Buddhist temple complex featuring a five-story pagoda, elaborate halls, and a peaceful garden with walking trails.
Visit artisan shops along the pedestrian streets to taste freshly made maple leaf-shaped cakes, Miyajima's signature souvenir filled with red bean paste or chocolate.
Tour this historic building with tatami mats covering 857 square meters where feudal lords once held ceremonies and viewed the torii gate.
View historical artifacts and national treasures including ancient scrolls, armor, and religious objects in this museum adjacent to the shrine.

food.detail: Miyajima's iconic maple leaf-shaped sponge cake traditionally filled with sweet red bean paste, often enjoyed fresh from local bakeries.
Grilled conger eel glazed with a sweet soy sauce served over rice, a local specialty that highlights the island's seafood traditions.
Savory layered pancake made with cabbage, meat, noodles, and topped with okonomiyaki sauce and bonito flakes, a regional specialty from nearby Hiroshima.
Fresh Hiroshima oysters grilled in the shell over charcoal and seasoned simply with salt or soy sauce, reflecting the region's famous oyster harvests.
Sample local sake from breweries in the Akitsu area connected by ferry, featuring varieties crafted with water from the Seto Inland Sea region.

culture.text: Respect Tide Times for Torii Photography
culture.detail: Plan your shrine visit around high and low tides; the torii gate's appearance changes dramatically and visiting at high tide offers the famous 'floating' effect.
Remove shoes when entering temple buildings and halls, and avoid wearing revealing clothing as a sign of respect in these sacred spaces.
The island's semi-wild deer are considered sacred; feeding them encourages aggressive behavior and disrupts their natural diet, so observe from a distance instead.
Miyajima attracts thousands of daily visitors; arriving at dawn or visiting in shoulder seasons provides a more peaceful and authentic experience of the shrine.

places.name: Okayama
places.description: Known for Okayama Castle and the stunning Korakuen Garden, one of Japan's three great gardens with carefully designed landscapes and seasonal beauty. The city serves as a gateway to exploring the Seto Inland Sea and offers excellent local cuisine and cultural experiences.

activities.detail: Explore this striking black feudal castle, reconstructed in 1966, which offers panoramic views of the city and the Seto Inland Sea from its upper floors.
Wander through one of Japan's three great gardens featuring carefully designed landscapes, tea houses, and seasonal displays that change throughout the year.
Take a ferry from Okayama to Naoshima, famous for contemporary art museums and outdoor installations set against scenic island landscapes.
Discover Islamic ceramics, glass, and metalwork spanning centuries in this museum dedicated to the art and culture of the Islamic world.
View Japanese painting, calligraphy, and decorative arts from the medieval period to contemporary works in a modern museum setting.
Visit this ancient shrine known for its unique shuttle-shaped ema (prayer plaques) and atmospheric approach lined with stone lanterns and forested paths.
Rent a bicycle and explore the scenic islands and coastal routes of the Seto Inland Sea, connecting small villages and beach communities.

food.detail: Savor locally distinctive ramen topped with pork bone-based broth, typically garnished with kikurage (wood ear mushrooms) and distinctive local variations.
Try this local scattered sushi dish where seasoned rice is topped with colorful ingredients like chirashi-style vegetables, egg, and seafood in a single bowl.
Sample these soft, mochi-like dumplings made from kibi (millet) flour and filled with sweet red bean paste, a famous Okayama souvenir.
Taste Okayama's prized white peaches, known for exceptional sweetness and delicate flavor, best enjoyed fresh during summer season.
Enjoy fresh oysters harvested from the nearby Hinase area, grilled, raw, or steamed, showcasing the region's coastal bounty.
Visit traditional sweet shops like Kibidango-no-Furusato to experience freshly made kibidango with green tea in their small, historic establishments.

culture.detail: When visiting shrines like Kibitsu, remove shoes before entering buildings, bow at torii gates, and respect quiet spaces—many visitors write wishes on ema plaques.
Japanese gardens like Korakuen are designed for seasonal viewing; visiting in spring (cherry blossoms), summer, autumn, or winter each reveals different designed aesthetics and moods.
Purchase a Okayama IC card or day pass for seamless travel on local buses and trams; many tourist attractions offer discounted combo tickets when purchased together.
Check ferry schedules in advance to Naoshima and other Seto Inland Sea islands, as services are less frequent than urban transit and weather can affect departures.

places.name: Kurashiki
places.description: A beautifully preserved merchant town with a picturesque canal-side district featuring white-walled warehouses converted into museums, galleries, and cafes. The charming Bikan district transports visitors to the Edo period while hosting world-class art museums and local craft shops.

activities.detail: Japan's first private art museum housing European masterpieces, Japanese works, and contemporary pieces in a striking white-walled building.
Stroll the picturesque waterway lined with 200-year-old white-walled warehouses now converted into galleries, cafes, and boutique shops.
Explore traditional Japanese mingei (folk crafts) displayed in a restored Edo-period merchant house with textiles, ceramics, and woodwork.
A restored red-brick textile factory complex from the Meiji era now hosting shops, restaurants, and galleries with romantic ivy-covered architecture.
A charming collection of vintage handmade toys and folk crafts from across Japan displayed in traditional warehouse settings.
A small but historically significant shrine overlooking the Bikan district, offering views of the canal and surrounding rooftops.

food.detail: Tender chicken grilled with soy-based tare sauce, a regional specialty reflecting Okayama Prefecture's culinary tradition.

food.text: Kakigori shaved ice with kuromame syrup
food.detail: Finely shaved ice topped with sweet black soybean syrup, a refreshing summer treat popular in the historic district's cafes.
A local slow-cooked hotpot featuring seasonal vegetables, tofu, and fish cakes simmered in rich dashi broth served at traditional restaurants.
Local confections highlighting Okayama's famous white peaches, sold as fresh treats or packed in traditional wagashi-style cakes.
A nearby regional ramen style featuring seafood-based broth, reflecting the proximity to coastal Kasaoka town.

culture.detail: Many converted warehouses are working galleries and shops with narrow passageways; move slowly, speak quietly, and purchase to support artisans.

culture.text: Visit early morning or late afternoon
culture.detail: The Bikan district becomes crowded with tour groups mid-day, so arrive by 8 AM or after 4 PM for a more authentic atmosphere.
The entire historic district is best explored on foot with uneven stone paths and traditional stepping stones, so sturdy footwear is essential.
Always ask permission before photographing inside galleries and craft shops, as many artists and owners prefer privacy for their displays.

places.name: Matsue
places.description: Home to the beautifully preserved Matsue Castle, one of Japan's finest remaining original castles surrounded by scenic moats and gardens. The city is also famous for Lake Shinji views, samurai districts, and as a gateway to the Izumo region's mythological sites.

activities.detail: Climb the five-story original keep of this beautifully preserved 17th-century castle, one of Japan's dozen remaining original structures, with views over the surrounding moat and city.
Watch the sun descend over Japan's seventh-largest lake from the waterfront promenade or a scenic spot like Yomegashira, a natural vantage point famous among locals for its reflection views.
Stroll the preserved samurai neighborhoods with traditional wooden townhouses, stone walls, and period gardens, particularly around Tanabe and Shiomi areas near the castle.
Visit the ancient Shinto shrine dedicated to Okuninushi, one of Japan's most important spiritual sites, located an hour west in the Izumo region where gods are said to gather.
Explore this world-class museum featuring Japanese paintings, calligraphy, and a stunning dry landscape garden designed as a living artwork, located 25 minutes south of central Matsue.
Walk among hundreds of bird species in Japanese and international aviaries, set within landscaped gardens along the Ohashigawa River.
Browse traditional Matsue-ori and Sekishu-washi textiles, along with lacquered tea bowls and decorative items in the historic merchant quarter.

food.detail: Savor locally-raised high-quality beef grilled at yakitori or yakiniku restaurants, prized for its marbling and tender texture from the prefecture's pristine grazing lands.
Taste Lake Shinji's signature small freshwater clams in a delicate, umami-rich broth that locals eat for breakfast or as a side dish at traditional restaurants.
Sample the region's famous buckwheat noodles served with a darker, richer soy-based sauce and often topped with grated radish, eaten throughout Matsue and the Izumo area.
Enjoy freshly grilled saltwater conger eel glazed with sweet sauce served over warm rice, a speciality of Matsue's riverside restaurants.
Try this warm red bean sweet soup with mochi, traditionally served at tea houses and street stalls, especially popular during cooler seasons.
Experience a hearty hot pot meal originally eaten by sumo wrestlers, featuring local vegetables, tofu, and meat simmered together at table-side restaurants.

culture.text: Respect the Sacred Seasons at Izumo Taisha
culture.detail: The Shinto deity Okuninushi is believed to gather at Izumo Taisha during October (called 'Kamiariwizuki' in the region), so dress modestly and participate quietly in rituals.

culture.text: Wear Comfortable Shoes for Castle Exploration
culture.detail: Matsue Castle's interior features steep wooden stairs and narrow passages requiring either careful climbing or removal of shoes; flat soles or slip-ons are practical.
When visiting temples and shrines, bow before passing through torii gates, rinse hands and mouth at water basins, and avoid pointing or loud conversation in sacred spaces.

culture.text: Best Visiting Months Are Spring and Autumn
culture.detail: Cherry blossom season (late March-April) and autumn foliage (October-November) draw crowds but offer the most pleasant weather; summer is humid and winters can be rainy.

places.name: Naagi
places.description: Home to the spectacular Nachi Falls (one of Japan's most dramatic waterfalls) and the ancient Kumano Nachi Taisha shrine perched on the mountainside. This spiritual destination combines natural beauty with deep religious significance along the UNESCO Kumano Kodo pilgrimage routes.

activities.detail: Experience Japan's tallest single-drop waterfall at 133 meters, best viewed from the dedicated observation platform where mist and spray create a mesmerizing atmosphere.
Explore this ancient shrine perched on the mountainside overlooking Nachi Falls, one of the three grand shrines of Kumano with over 1,400 years of spiritual heritage.
Trek portions of the UNESCO-registered pilgrimage routes that connect sacred sites through pristine forest and mountain terrain, ranging from 2 to 8 hours depending on the chosen route.
Visit this unique three-story pagoda standing directly against the backdrop of Nachi Falls, creating one of Japan's most photographed compositions of spirituality and nature.
Discover this lesser-known 100-meter waterfall hidden in the mountains near Nachi, accessible via a scenic hiking trail through old-growth forest.
Practice shinrin-yoku (forest bathing) along the quiet woodland paths of the Kumano region, immersing yourself in the therapeutic atmosphere of ancient cedar and cypress groves.

food.detail: A local pressed sushi specialty where vinegared rice and local fish are compressed in wooden molds, reflecting Kumano's mountainous geography where fresh sashimi was historically difficult to obtain.
Sushi wrapped in pickled persimmon leaves, a Wakayama regional specialty that adds subtle flavor and aroma while preserving the rice in the humid mountain climate.
Nachi's coastal waters have a centuries-old whaling tradition; sample grilled or simmered whale at local restaurants, experiencing this controversial but historically significant local protein.
Wakayama prefecture is famous for pickled plums; seek out local varieties in Nachi where traditional ume farming continues in the surrounding valleys.
A creamy sesame-based ramen unique to the region, often served with local mountain vegetables and reflecting the spiritual vegetarian traditions of the shrine pilgrims.

culture.detail: When visiting Kumano Nachi Taisha, bow before passing through torii gates, rinse hands and mouth at purification basins, and remove shoes when entering sacred indoor spaces.

culture.text: Pilgrimage is spiritual, not just hiking
culture.detail: The Kumano Kodo routes are sacred pilgrimage paths where locals and visitors traditionally walk for spiritual cleansing; approach with meditative intent rather than purely recreational hiking mindset.
Visit Nachi Falls and the shrine in early morning when mist rises from the forest and crowds are minimal, honoring the contemplative atmosphere the sacred sites are meant to inspire.
Purchase a goshuin-chō (pilgrimage record book) at the shrine to collect official stamps at each sacred site, a meaningful tradition that connects you to centuries of pilgrims.

places.name: Onomichi
places.description: A nostalgic port town beloved by filmmakers and artists, featuring narrow lanes lined with temples, traditional shops, and atmospheric hillside streets. The town offers stunning views of the Seto Inland Sea and easy access to Shimanami Kaido, a famous cycling route connecting nearby islands.

activities.detail: Explore the hillside path connecting 25 temples, including the iconic Senkoji Temple with its red lantern and sweeping sea views.
Rent a bicycle and ride the scenic 70 km route across six islands connected by bridges, starting from Onomichi's waterfront.
See contemporary and traditional artworks in a museum that reflects the town's long history as a muse for painters and filmmakers.
Stroll narrow lanes like Sandō-shotengai where wooden machiya buildings house vintage shops, cafes, and art galleries.

activities.text: Take a Seto Inland Sea Ferry Ride
activities.detail: Board a local ferry to appreciate the archipelago's landscape and stop at nearby islands like Innoshima or Mukaishima.
Follow marked routes to buildings and streets featured in Japanese New Wave cinema, including works by Masaki Kobayashi and Nobuhiko Ōbayashi.

food.detail: Local specialty with a rich pork-and-fish-based broth, topped with pork belly, shallots, and a soft-boiled egg, served at family-run noodle shops.
Octopus-filled battered balls sold from historic street stalls, crispy outside and creamy inside, a quick snack as you navigate the lanes.
Waterfront restaurants serve seasonal seafood—flounder, sea bream, and squid—caught daily from the surrounding waters.
Deep-fried skewered vegetables and meats dipped in a shared tonkatsu sauce, a casual dining tradition at izakayas throughout town.
Traditional red-bean filled steamed cakes with a soft, pillowy texture, sold at small confectioneries as a local souvenir.

culture.detail: Many temples charge a small entry fee; remove shoes before entering buildings and bow respectfully when passing through torii gates.
Visit early morning or on weekdays to enjoy the atmospheric narrow lanes without the mid-day tourist rush that peaks in late afternoon.
If cycling the Shimanami Kaido, stay on designated paths, yield to pedestrians, and return rentals to designated shops—cycling etiquette is taken seriously.
Many family-run galleries and shops in traditional buildings welcome browsers; purchasing directly from makers preserves the town's creative culture.

places.name: Hagi
places.description: A beautifully preserved castle town featuring samurai and merchant quarters with traditional architecture, famous for its ceramics heritage and Hagi-yaki pottery. The town offers a glimpse into Edo-period life with museums, traditional crafts workshops, and coastal scenery.

activities.detail: Explore the remains of this 17th-century castle set on a hilltop overlooking the town, with reconstructed gates and pleasant walking paths through the grounds.
Tour working kilns and pottery workshops throughout town where artisans create traditional Hagi-yaki ceramics using local clay, often with opportunities to watch or try your hand at the wheel.

activities.text: Samurai and Merchant Quarter Walking Tour
activities.detail: Stroll through beautifully preserved streets lined with whitewashed merchant houses, samurai residences, and traditional wooden buildings that authentically represent Edo-period urban life.
This museum houses one of Japan's finest collections of Hagi ceramics spanning centuries, displayed in a modern building overlooking the coastal landscape.
Walk along the rocky shoreline and beaches near the town center for views of the Nihon Kai Sea and traditional fishing villages nestled between mountains and water.

Visit a narrow lane lined with several Buddhist temples, including the historic Shoin-ji and Koin-ji, offering peaceful atmosphere and spiritual atmosphere distinct from the town's secular attractions.

food.detail: A multi-course meal served in locally made Hagi ceramic dishes, emphasizing seasonal ingredients and the tactile beauty of eating from hand-thrown pottery.
Premium grilled or hotpot beef from nearby Tokushima Prefecture, featuring tender marbling and rich flavor, often featured at upscale local restaurants.
Fresh sashimi and sushi featuring catches from the Nihon Kai Sea, particularly sweet shrimp and seasonal white fish prepared by masters in traditional establishments.
Sweet, plump oysters harvested from local coastal waters, typically enjoyed grilled with salt or in light broths during autumn and winter months.
Sample junmai daiginjo and other sake styles from Hagi's historic sake breweries, which have operated for over two centuries using local spring water.
Thick, chewy wheat noodles in a mild broth made with dashi from local fish, often topped with vegetables and served in traditional ceramic bowls.

culture.detail: Ceramics are central to Hagi's identity—pieces are intentionally rustic with natural glaze irregularities, so embrace imperfection as beauty rather than expecting polished uniformity.

When visiting active kilns and workshops, ask permission before photographing artists at work, remove shoes when entering traditional spaces, and purchase something if you spend extended time observing.
Spring (cherry blossoms) and autumn offer ideal weather for walking the castle grounds and merchant streets, while winter oyster season is celebrated but brings cooler, windier coastal conditions.
English signage is minimal compared to major cities, so obtaining a detailed map with Japanese characters or hiring a local guide enhances understanding of historical context and pottery traditions.

places.name: Iwakuni
places.description: Famous for the iconic Kintai Bridge, a stunning arched wooden bridge spanning the Nishiki River with distinctive wooden construction dating back centuries. The town blends historical significance with natural beauty and offers access to Iwakuni Castle with sweeping river valley views.

activities.detail: Cross the iconic five-arched wooden bridge spanning the Nishiki River, admiring its traditional construction and enjoying views of the surrounding landscape.
Visit the reconstructed hilltop castle offering panoramic views of the Nishiki River valley and access to samurai history exhibits.
Walk through this riverside park featuring seasonal cherry blossoms, maple trees, and traditional Japanese landscaping adjacent to Kintai Bridge.
Explore local and regional art collections housed in a modern building overlooking the Nishiki River valley.
Trek through forested paths to reach an elevated shrine with commanding views of the river and surrounding town.
Experience the traditional summer evening spectacle of ukai (cormorant fishing) on the Nishiki River with trained birds.

food.detail: A local specialty featuring sushi pressed into a wooden box with layers of vinegared rice, fish, and vegetables in a distinctive rectangular presentation.
Fresh river ayu from the Nishiki River grilled whole over charcoal, prized for its delicate sweetness and tender texture.
A warming rice bowl topped with ground chicken, egg, and seasonal vegetables unique to Iwakuni's local culinary tradition.

food.text: Aka miso (red miso) from local makers
food.detail: Iwakuni's distinctive rich and deeply flavored red miso, available at specialty shops and used in local cooking.
Grilled freshwater eel prepared as kabayaki or in donburi, taking advantage of the river's pristine water and eel populations.

culture.detail: Walk mindfully across Kintai Bridge and avoid stopping in the center during peak hours; locals and regular visitors maintain steady foot traffic.
Spring brings cherry blossoms and autumn showcases brilliant maple foliage, making these peak seasons for crowds but offering spectacular natural beauty.
Understanding Iwakuni's role as a castle town under the Kikkawa clan enhances appreciation of local museums, shrines, and architectural details throughout town.
Support local artisans by seeking out handmade wooden crafts and bridge-themed souvenirs rather than mass-produced items in tourist shops.

places.name: Seto Inland Sea Islands
places.description: A collection of picturesque islands scattered across the Seto Inland Sea, including Naoshima and Teshima known for world-class contemporary art museums and installations. The islands offer scenic ferry journeys, beach access, and a peaceful escape combining natural beauty with cutting-edge cultural experiences.

activities.detail: A world-renowned contemporary art museum on Naoshima showcasing cutting-edge installations and works by international artists in a striking architectural setting.
An underground art museum on Naoshima featuring mostly natural light and earth tones, housing major works by Monet, James Turrell, and Walter De Maria.
A minimalist architectural masterpiece on Teshima designed by Tadao Ando, housing a single major contemporary art installation in a serene hilltop setting.
Scenic boat rides connecting the main islands offer stunning views of the Seto Inland Sea's characteristic pine-dotted landscape and traditional fishing villages.
Explore the quieter Kasaoka Islands cluster with bicycles and local ferries, visiting small shrines, sandy beaches, and rural communities untouched by major tourism.
Stroll through this traditional port town on the Hiroshima mainland with temple-filled hillsides, narrow lanes, and well-preserved wooden merchant houses dating back centuries.
Access pristine sandy beaches on the quieter southern islands, ideal for swimming, picnicking, and experiencing the calm waters of the inland sea.

food.detail: Fresh grilled or sashimi preparation of the region's prized seabream, a local specialty caught daily in these waters and served at waterfront restaurants.
The Hiroshima-style layered savory pancake with cabbage, proteins, and sauce is widely available in port towns and island restaurants as a hearty local comfort food.
Tiny freshly-caught whitebait served warm over steamed rice with soy sauce and seaweed, a signature dish at casual island eateries celebrating the sea's bounty.

food.text: Kakigori ice shaved with island citrus
food.detail: Summer refreshment made from shaved ice topped with locally-produced lemon, yuzu, or bitter orange syrup reflecting the islands' citrus agriculture.
Some islands offer visitors the chance to harvest and taste freshly-picked nori seaweed sheets, understanding the traditional harvesting methods still used today.

culture.detail: Island ferries run on fixed schedules with reduced service in winter and evenings, so plan activities and accommodation with departure times in mind to avoid being stranded.
Most islands lack public transport; rent a bicycle at ferry terminals to navigate villages, beaches, and art sites at a pace that suits island life.
These are still working communities—keep noise low, avoid photographing residents without permission, and show reverence at active shrines and temples.
Many small restaurants, shops, and accommodations on islands do not accept cards, so withdraw sufficient yen at Onomichi or larger towns before island visits.

places.name: Kure
places.description: A maritime city housing Japan's fascinating naval history museum and the preserved battleship Yamato Memorial Museum, documenting the warship's history. The port city offers excellent fresh seafood, maritime heritage sites, and serves as a practical base for exploring nearby Hiroshima region attractions.

activities.detail: Explore the history of Japan's largest battleship through detailed exhibits, scale models, and immersive displays documenting the Yamato's construction and final mission.
Tour Japan's official naval museum featuring submarines, destroyers, and extensive exhibits on naval warfare and maritime defense history.
Stroll along the revitalized port area with views of active naval vessels, modern waterfront facilities, and scenic harbor photography opportunities.
View contemporary Japanese art and rotating exhibitions in this modern museum located near the city center with focus on regional artists.
Visit this scenic park with recreational facilities, walking paths along the Seto Inland Sea, and seasonal cherry blossoms in spring.
Board and explore the decommissioned submarine Akatsuki to experience submarine crew life and Cold War-era naval technology.

food.detail: A local specialty curry dish often served over rice with tender pork or seafood, reflecting the city's naval heritage and becoming a signature comfort food.
Fresh oysters from nearby Hiroshima Bay served grilled, raw, or in hot pots during winter months, showcasing the region's premier shellfish harvesting.
Grilled or steamed conger eel served over rice or as sashimi, a regional delicacy prepared with traditional Seto Inland Sea techniques.
Hiroshima-style savory pancakes with layered ingredients including noodles, cabbage, and protein, reflecting the broader Chūgoku region's culinary tradition.
Daily catches of local seafood served at harbor-side restaurants featuring seasonal fish, squid, and shellfish at peak freshness.

culture.detail: Visitors should approach Yamato Museum and naval sites with respectful demeanor as these are solemn memorials to fallen sailors and significant wartime history.
Kure maintains active military and civilian shipping operations, so visiting the waterfront early morning offers the best views of naval activity and fewer crowds.
Locals speak Hiroshima-ben dialect which differs noticeably from standard Japanese; patience and a smile help when communicating with older shopkeepers and restaurant staff.
As a coastal port city, Kure can experience typhoons from July through September; check weather forecasts and heed local advisories during these months.

places.name: Takayama
places.description: Though in Gifu, this mountain town is essential for Chugoku travelers seeking traditional Japanese character with beautifully preserved merchant houses and sake breweries. The town features morning markets, mountain scenery, and provides access to Shirakawa-go village for deep cultural immersion.

activities.detail: Walk through three charming streets lined with beautifully preserved merchant houses, sake breweries, and traditional shops dating back to the Edo period.
Tour the former government administrative building showcasing authentic samurai-era architecture and period furnishings from the Edo and Meiji periods.
Shop at two historic morning markets—Jinya-mae and Miyagawa—where local farmers sell fresh vegetables, fruits, flowers, and handcrafted goods.
Visit working sake breweries like Sakurayama Hanamura or Fujita Sake Brewery to learn traditional brewing methods and sample premium local sake.
Explore an open-air museum with over 30 traditional farmhouses relocated from surrounding villages, offering insight into rural mountain life.
Step back into 1960s Japan with this quirky museum featuring vintage toys, household items, and nostalgic memorabilia filling three restored wooden buildings.
Take a day trip 50 kilometers northwest to this UNESCO World Heritage village famous for gassho-zukuri farmhouses with steep thatched roofs.

food.detail: Sample grilled or simmered premium wagyu from Hida region cattle, known for marbling and tenderness, served at upscale restaurants and casual grills.
Try local ramen with thin noodles in a rich, soy-based broth often topped with chashu pork, pickled vegetables, and a raw egg.
Taste lightly battered and deep-fried wild mountain vegetables and edible plants unique to the Hida highlands.
Enjoy grilled rice cakes coated with a savory miso-walnut sauce, a regional snack sold at morning markets and traditional shops.
Participate in guided tastings at breweries sampling junmai, ginjo, and aged varieties paired with local sake snacks and pickles.
Try miso paste grilled on a large magnolia leaf with vegetables and mushrooms, a traditional dish served in local restaurants and at lunch sets.

culture.detail: Jinya-mae and Miyagawa markets begin at dawn and are liveliest before 9 AM, so plan morning visits to see the best selection and energy.
When visiting sake breweries, remove shoes or wear provided slippers, ask before photographing, and purchase something if sampling is offered.
English is limited outside main tourist areas; learning 'arigatou gozaimasu' (thank you), 'onegaishimasu' (please), and 'sumimasen' (excuse me) greatly enriches interactions.
Visit October through November for clear mountain scenery and sake season, or December through February to experience heavy snow and fewer crowds.

restaurants.name: Okonomiyaki Kiji
restaurants.description: Historic okonomiyaki restaurant established in 1950, famous for its layered Hiroshima-style preparation with noodles and egg.
restaurants.mustOrder: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with pork and squid

restaurants.name: Sushidai
restaurants.description: Intimate sushi counter near Hiroshima port featuring fresh daily catches and traditional Edomae techniques.

restaurants.name: Ajisai
restaurants.description: Contemporary Japanese cuisine blending traditional flavors with modern plating in the heart of downtown Hiroshima.

restaurants.name: Kushikatsu Daruma
restaurants.description: Casual counter-style restaurant serving crispy deep-fried skewers with tangy dipping sauce, local favorite since 1999.

restaurants.name: Onomichi Ramen Yokocho
restaurants.description: Atmospheric alley with eight small ramen shops, each serving Onomichi's signature style with soy broth and local toppings.
restaurants.mustOrder: Onomichi ramen with grilled fish cake

restaurants.name: Imaasahi
restaurants.description: Waterfront izakaya specializing in fresh local catch from the Seto Inland Sea with seasonal preparations.

restaurants.name: Tatsuta
restaurants.description: Fine dining restaurant offering elaborate seasonal kaiseki meals using premium local ingredients in an elegant traditional setting.

restaurants.name: Donki Hote
restaurants.description: Local institution famous for thick-cut tonkatsu breaded and fried to crispy perfection, served with special sauce.

restaurants.name: Agarikiya
restaurants.description: Charming restaurant in historic Bikan district serving traditional Kurashiki dishes in a restored warehouse setting.

restaurants.name: Beni-Shoga
restaurants.description: Premium yakiniku restaurant specializing in Hida beef, one of Japan's finest wagyu varieties, grilled tableside.
restaurants.mustOrder: Hida beef sirloin and ribeye selection

restaurants.name: Syunsai Yoshida
restaurants.description: Refined kaiseki dining overlooking Lake Shinji, featuring seasonal local ingredients and Lake Shinji's famous shijimi clams.
restaurants.mustOrder: Shijimi clam soup with seasonal kaiseki

restaurants.name: Ichidai
restaurants.description: Specialized restaurant serving Tottori's prized snow crabs prepared steamed, grilled, and in broths during winter season.

restaurants.name: Kamoizumi Brewery Restaurant
restaurants.description: Restaurant attached to historic sake brewery offering local dishes paired with freshly brewed sake varieties.
restaurants.mustOrder: Sake-steamed chicken with fresh sake pairing

restaurants.name: Ikkyoan
restaurants.description: Small legendary ramen shop in Onomichi serving rich soy-based broth with distinctive local char siu and fish cake.

regions/shikoku.json.name: Shikoku
regions/shikoku.json.history: Shikoku, Japan's smallest main island, has been inhabited since prehistoric times and developed as an agricultural center during the feudal period under various daimyo rulers. The island was historically divided into four provinces, which is reflected in its name meaning 'four countries.' During the Edo period, Shikoku remained relatively isolated from central Japanese politics, allowing its local culture and traditions to flourish independently. The modern era brought industrial development and improved transportation links, though the island has maintained much of its traditional character compared to more urbanized regions.
regions/shikoku.json.culturalNotes: Shikoku is most famous as the home of the Shikoku Pilgrimage, a 1,200-kilometer sacred Buddhist circuit visiting 88 temples that has attracted pilgrims for centuries and remains a deeply spiritual journey. The island is known for its distinctive Awa Odori dance festival in Tokushima, featuring energetic group performances that draw thousands of visitors annually. Traditional crafts such as indigo dyeing and pottery production continue to thrive in local communities, reflecting Shikoku's strong connection to artisanal heritage and rural lifestyles.
regions/shikoku.json.bestTimeToVisit: October through November provides pleasant temperatures and clear skies.
regions/shikoku.json.languageNotes: The Shikoku dialect, particularly the Awa dialect of Tokushima, preserves older Japanese linguistic features and can be quite distinct from standard Japanese. Useful phrases include 'Awa Odori e iku' (going to see the Awa Odori) and 'henro michi' (pilgrimage trail), terms deeply embedded in local culture and conversation.

places.name: Kochi
places.description: The largest city in Shikoku, known for its historic castle, lively Sunday market, and gateway to the island's cultural attractions. The castle is one of Japan's finest remaining feudal structures and offers sweeping views of the city.

activities.detail: Explore one of Japan's finest remaining feudal castles, built in 1603, featuring original wooden structures and panoramic city views from the keep.
Browse the lively open-air market that has operated every Sunday for nearly 300 years, selling local produce, crafts, and street food along Otsuji Street.
